World 1 · Chapter 24

Solids and Volume

How to measure the space a three-dimensional shape contains

35 min foundational Greece, Sicily, Italy Ancient Greece, Renaissance

Objectives

  • Understand the difference between 2D shapes and 3D solids.
  • Calculate volume for rectangular prisms, cylinders, cones, and spheres.
  • Relate surface area to volume.
  • Know the five Platonic solids and why there are only five.
  • Apply Euler's formula to solids.

On This Page

  • Story Hook
  • Why It Matters
  • Historical Context
  • Core Concept
  • Definitions and Formal Lesson

  • Key Parts of a Solid
  • Euler's Formula
  • Volume Formulas
  • The Sphere-Cylinder Relationship

  • Example 2: Volume and Surface Area of a Cylinder

  • Unlock Quiz
  • Source Notes

Story Hook

Archimedes was given a problem that seemed simple but led him to one of his greatest discoveries. King Hiero II had ordered a golden crown, but he suspected the goldsmith of cheating—mixing silver in with the gold. The king asked Archimedes: can you tell me if the crown is pure gold without melting it?

Archimedes spent days thinking. Then, while lowering himself into a bath, he noticed the water displaced by his body. In that moment, he realized he could measure the crown's volume by water displacement. He knew gold's density. If the crown's weight-to-volume ratio matched gold's, it was pure. If not, silver had been mixed in.

He leaped from the bath and ran through the streets shouting "Eureka!"—"I have found it!" But his discovery was about something deeper than catching thieves. He had discovered that volume—the space a solid contains—could be measured precisely. And later, he would prove something that astonished even him: that a sphere and a cylinder fit together in a perfect mathematical relationship.

Why It Matters

Volume matters because it answers the question: how much space does a 3D object occupy?

This matters because:

  • builders must calculate how much concrete to pour, how much water a pool holds, how much air a room contains
  • manufacturers need to know how much material to use and how much packaging space they need
  • architects balance solid structure with interior space
  • chemists and physicists measure volumes of liquids and gases
  • Archimedes' discovery of the sphere-to-cylinder relationship revolutionized geometry and showed that pure reasoning could uncover nature's deepest harmonies

Understanding solids and volume is the bridge from flat geometry to the three-dimensional world.

Historical Context

Plato, in his philosophical writings, reflected on five special solids—what we now call the Platonic solids. He associated four of them with the classical elements: tetrahedron (fire), cube (earth), octahedron (air), and icosahedron (water). The fifth, the dodecahedron, he associated with the universe as a whole. Plato did not discover these solids—Pythagoras knew them—but his writings helped preserve their importance.

Archimedes of Syracuse (c. 287–212 BCE) made extraordinary discoveries about volume. He proved that a sphere's volume is exactly two-thirds the volume of the cylinder that perfectly surrounds it—a fact he found so beautiful that he requested it be inscribed on his tomb. He also found the surface area of a sphere and proved formulas for volumes of cones and other solids. Much of what he wrote was lost, but what survives shows a mind of unmatched precision.

In the Renaissance, Johannes Kepler, the great astronomer, wrote Mysterium Cosmographicum (The Cosmic Mystery), in which he speculated that the Platonic solids had a role in the structure of the universe—fitting between the orbits of the planets. This was incorrect, but it shows how deeply mathematicians revered these five solids.

Core Concept

A solid is a three-dimensional shape that occupies space. Solids have faces (flat surfaces), edges (where faces meet), and vertices (corners where edges meet).

Volume is the amount of space a solid contains, measured in cubic units (cm³, m³, etc.).

Surface area is the total area of all the faces of a solid.

The five Platonic solids are the only regular solids where all faces are identical regular polygons and all vertices look the same. There are exactly five:

  1. Tetrahedron (4 triangular faces)
  2. Cube (6 square faces)
  3. Octahedron (8 triangular faces)
  4. Dodecahedron (12 pentagonal faces)
  5. Icosahedron (20 triangular faces)

Definitions and Formal Lesson

Solid Types

Prism A solid with two parallel, congruent faces (bases) and rectangular side faces. A rectangular prism is a box. A triangular prism has triangular bases.

Pyramid A solid with a polygon as a base and triangular faces that meet at a point (apex) above the base.

Cylinder A solid with two parallel, congruent circular bases and a curved side surface.

Cone A solid with a circular base and a curved surface that tapers to a point (apex) above the base.

Sphere A perfectly round solid where every point on the surface is the same distance from the center.

Key Parts of a Solid

Face A flat surface of a solid. A cube has 6 faces.

Edge A line segment where two faces meet. A cube has 12 edges.

Vertex A corner point where three or more edges meet. A cube has 8 vertices.

Euler's Formula

For any convex polyhedron:

F + V − E = 2

where F = number of faces, V = number of vertices, and E = number of edges.

For a cube: F = 6, V = 8, E = 12 → 6 + 8 − 12 = 2 ✓

Volume Formulas

Rectangular Prism (Box) V = l × w × h (length × width × height)

Cylinder V = πr²h (π × radius² × height)

Cone V = (1/3)πr²h (one-third × π × radius² × height)

Sphere V = (4/3)πr³ (four-thirds × π × radius³)

Pyramid V = (1/3) × base area × height

The Sphere-Cylinder Relationship

One of Archimedes' greatest discoveries:

If a sphere of radius r is inscribed in a cylinder (touching the base, top, and curved side), then:

  • Cylinder volume = πr² × (2r) = 2πr³
  • Sphere volume = (4/3)πr³

The sphere's volume is exactly 2/3 of the cylinder's volume.

Worked Examples

Example 1: Volume of a Rectangular Prism

A rectangular storage box has dimensions 5 cm long, 3 cm wide, and 4 cm tall. How much volume can it hold?

Solution

Use the formula: V = l × w × h

V = 5 × 3 × 4 = 60 cm³

The box holds 60 cubic centimeters.

Example 2: Volume and Surface Area of a Cylinder

A cylindrical water tank has a radius of 2 meters and a height of 5 meters. Find its volume and surface area.

Solution

Volume:

V = πr²h = π × 2² × 5 = π × 4 × 5 = 20π m³ ≈ 62.83 m³

Surface area (two circular bases plus the curved side):

Area of two bases = 2 × πr² = 2 × π × 4 = 8π m² Curved side area = 2πrh = 2 × π × 2 × 5 = 20π m² Total surface area = 8π + 20π = 28π m² ≈ 87.96 m²

Example 3: Volume of a Cone

A cone-shaped party hat has a radius of 6 cm and a height of 15 cm. What is its volume?

Solution

Use the formula: V = (1/3)πr²h

V = (1/3) × π × 6² × 15 V = (1/3) × π × 36 × 15 V = (1/3) × 540π V = 180π cm³ ≈ 565.49 cm³

Example 4: Check Euler's Formula

A tetrahedron (one of the Platonic solids) has 4 faces, 4 vertices, and 6 edges. Verify Euler's formula.

Solution

F + V − E = 2 4 + 4 − 6 = 2 ✓

Euler's formula holds.

Practice Set

Warm-Up

  1. A cube has sides of length 3 cm. What is its volume?
  2. How many faces, edges, and vertices does a cube have?

Core Practice

  1. A sphere has a radius of 5 cm. Use the formula V = (4/3)πr³ to find its volume.
  2. A cone has a radius of 4 m and a height of 9 m. Find its volume.
  3. A rectangular prism has length 7 cm, width 4 cm, and height 6 cm. Find both its volume and surface area.
  4. For an octahedron (a Platonic solid with 8 faces, 6 vertices, and 12 edges), verify that Euler's formula holds.

Stretch

  1. A cylinder has radius 3 cm and height 6 cm. Its volume is πr²h. A cone with the same radius and height has volume (1/3)πr²h. Explain the factor of 1/3 by thinking about how many cones would fit inside the cylinder.
Practice Answer Guide
  1. V = 3 × 3 × 3 = 27 cm³
  2. A cube has 6 faces, 12 edges, and 8 vertices.
  3. V = (4/3)π × 5³ = (4/3)π × 125 = (500/3)π cm³ ≈ 523.6 cm³
  4. V = (1/3)π × 4² × 9 = (1/3)π × 16 × 9 = 48π m³ ≈ 150.8 m³
  5. Volume: V = 7 × 4 × 6 = 168 cm³. Surface area: 2(7×4) + 2(7×6) + 2(4×6) = 56 + 84 + 48 = 188 cm²
  6. F + V − E = 8 + 6 − 12 = 2 ✓
  7. Three cones of the same radius and height as a cylinder fit exactly inside it. So the cone's volume is 1/3 of the cylinder's volume. (The exact packing can be visualized by imagining three identical cones filling a cylinder.)

Reasoning Task

Archimedes claimed that a sphere's volume is exactly 2/3 of the cylinder that surrounds it. Without doing calculations, write 5–7 sentences explaining:

  • What it would take to verify this claim experimentally (using water or sand)
  • Why an experimental check is different from a mathematical proof
  • Whether you think Archimedes' belief that this relationship was beautiful says something about mathematics itself

Application

Volume calculations are essential in countless real-world contexts:

Engineering and Construction Concrete for foundations is ordered by volume. Plumbers calculate pipe volumes. Architects design spaces by volume to ensure proper air flow and thermal efficiency.

Manufacturing Bottles, containers, and storage systems are designed based on volume. Paint, fuel, and chemicals are sold by volume.

Science Chemists measure liquid volumes for reactions. Biologists calculate cell and tissue volumes. Meteorologists measure atmospheric volumes of gases.

Medicine and Health Dosages of liquid medicine are measured by volume. Medical imaging measures organ volumes to assess health.

Archimedes' Legacy The method of water displacement, discovered in that bathtub moment, is still used to find the volume of irregular objects. When mathematical formulas don't apply, we turn to the principle Archimedes discovered: volume can be measured by how much space a solid takes up.

Recap

  • A solid is a 3D shape with faces, edges, and vertices.
  • Volume is the space a solid occupies, measured in cubic units.
  • Rectangular prism volume: V = l × w × h
  • Cylinder volume: V = πr²h
  • Cone volume: V = (1/3)πr²h
  • Sphere volume: V = (4/3)πr³
  • Euler's formula for polyhedra: F + V − E = 2
  • Five Platonic solids are the only regular solids with identical faces.
  • A sphere's volume is 2/3 the volume of the cylinder that surrounds it.

Unlock Quiz

  1. What is the volume of a cube with side length 4 cm?
  • 12 cm³
  • 16 cm³
  • 64 cm³
  • 256 cm³
  1. A cylinder has radius 3 m and height 10 m. Which formula gives its volume?
  • πr²h = π × 9 × 10
  • (1/3)πr²h
  • 2πrh
  • 4πr³
  1. How many Platonic solids are there?
  • 3
  • 4
  • 5
  • 6
  1. For a cone with radius 5 cm and height 12 cm, what is its volume?
  • 300π cm³
  • 100π cm³
  • 120π cm³
  • (1/3)π × 5 × 12 cm³
  1. In Euler's formula F + V − E = 2, what does E represent?
  • Faces
  • Edges
  • Empty space
  • Exterior angles
Unlock Quiz Answers
  1. 64 cm³
  2. πr²h = π × 9 × 10
  3. 5
  4. 100π cm³
  5. Edges

Source Notes

  • Archimedes — biography and overview of his mathematical and engineering achievements, including his work on spheres and cylinders
  • Platonic solid — detailed coverage of the five regular solids, their properties, and their historical significance
  • Volume — comprehensive reference on volume calculations and formulas for different solids
  • Euler's formula — explanation of the relationship between faces, vertices, and edges in polyhedra
  • Sphere — properties of spheres including the famous relationship between spheres and cylinders discovered by Archimedes
